name: Ecktoria
description:
Ecktoria

Key Demographic: Humans Capital City: Ecktoria (The City)

*{Entanglements

description[leftmargin=*, style=nextline] [To the West:] Kahfagia -- Lantern-law fleets shadow the grain ships; tariffs are a polite fiction. The Amber Palace and the Beacon Crown exchange spies, not ambassadors. [To the East:] Acasia -- The Broken Marches are a source of cheap mercenaries and cheaper grain. The Vigil has eyes in every free company camp. [To the North:] The Violet Steppes. Fortified towns and cities ward against Ykrul raids. An uneasy peace stands between the Imperial remnant and the native Vilikari. [To the North East:] Aeler -- Mountain passes choke with dwarvish tariffs; the True Masons hold the keys to the high roads. The Senate pays in iron, and the mountain remembers. [To the South:] The Amaranthine Sea -- The old imperial navy rots in harbor; Zakov pirates and Thepyrgos chain-towers contest the straits. The flame of the Everflame does not burn on salt water. description

*{Quick Reference Tagline: The flame remembers. The marble cracks. The law forgets --- but the statues watch.

quote Grand Magistrate (Elite): "The law is written in marble and witnessed by the flame. Every judgment echoes in the stones --- but the stones have grown quiet. The patricians do not listen. They have forgotten what the flame tastes like. They seal their windows with silk and call it governance." quote

quote Ferrymaster (Commoner): "I've seen the city burn three times. The marble remembers the heat. The flame remembers the lies. The rich have stopped looking at the flames. They only look at each other --- and at the cracks in their own mirrors. The fire isn't burning anymore, friend. It's smiling." quote

Genre / Mood: Imperial decay, bureaucratic horror, memory as weight, Renaissance decadence --- a city walled off from its own rot, where the wealthy dance while the poor cough ash.

Starting Location: The Coin-House floor, marble cracked with soot lines, where votive candles gutter beside melted icons. A factor with smoke-stained fingers offers a folded note. "The Lame King moves tonight. The Basilica burns with him. The Amber Palace hasn't answered a summons in a month. Find out who lit the fire --- or the Vigil will audit your soul at dawn. And stay away from the Arena. The statues have been whispering."

[colback=black!3,colframe=blue!60!black,title={Theme & Atmosphere,breakable] Once the heart of the Utaran Empire, Ecktoria has worn its laurels into chains. Three centuries of sack, fire, and slow decline have not stripped its marble forums nor its gold-sealed coin-houses --- but each day, more of its weight slips into the hands of mercenaries and whispers. The Black Banners march on its purse, avenging old Ykrul wounds by proxy. Processions still crown its stairways, yet ash lines every step. The city burns not only with memory, but with a grief that refuses to name itself.

Yet the true sickness is not in the streets. It is in the Amber Palace, where the old patrician families have walled themselves away. They sit on laurels preserved from the empire, drinking wine that costs a village's harvest, whispering deals with merchants who have never seen a loom. Maelstraeus, the demon of commerce, has threaded their accounts with invisible tolls; every contract they sign forgets a clause. Livaea, the demon of desire, has wrapped their hearts in silk; they marry for influence, divorce for scandal, and love only the reflection of themselves in gilded mirrors --- reflections that no longer blink. The common folk call them the Unburdened --- not because they carry no weight, but because they have forgotten what weight feels like.

The Vigil --- once the empire's secret police, now a fractured ghost of an institution --- fights a silent war over the dream of restoration. One faction, the Censer-Knights, serves the Everflame and the Synod, hunting heresy and latent magic with brass censers and bells that ring at dawn. Another, the Unburnt, answers only to the old patrician houses, ensuring that no whisper of rebellion reaches the Amber Palace. A third, the Red Vigil, has sold itself to Maelstraeus, auditing not coin but memory --- they collect the names of those who have forgotten what they owe. They do not fight each other openly. They meet in the hypogeum of the Arena, under the gaze of statues that weep marble tears. The statues are not decorative. They are the city's witnesses.

Beyond the capital's walls, the province is a patchwork of struggling towns, abandoned fortresses, and grainlands whose harvests are disputed by rival tax-collectors. The Everflame Orthodoxy preaches unity, but each sermon is a coded signal for a political camp. The statues watch. The fire judges. And in the sewers, the Vigil's informers listen for the name of the next person to vanish --- not into death, but into the margins of a record no one will ever read.

What you notice first: The smell of old incense and fresh bread --- and beneath it, the faint sweetness of rot. The way crowds part for a procession --- and close again for a knife. The marble steps, worn smooth by centuries of petitioners. The way the wealthy never step on the cracks. The cracks are where the petitions of the poor are wedged. The cracks are where the Hollow reads.

The one rule that kills newcomers: Never refuse a toast in a coin-house. The tallies are the true judges, and a declined cup is a declined trust. You will not borrow again. But more than that: never accept a gift from a patrician without naming its price aloud. Maelstraeus and Livaea both listen, and a gift accepted in silence is a contract written in water --- it will drown you eventually.

*{Regional Mood: The Flame That Remembers --- And The Dancers Who Forgot

Here, the past does not fade --- it is polished into monuments. Every statue, every obelisk, every triumphal arch is a legal precedent carved in stone. But precedent is not justice. Precedent is memory, and memory is a weight that the city carries like a cracked keel. Ecktoria is not one city but three: the marble forums of the old patricians (where the Unburdened hold masques that last for weeks, their faces painted with ash from the Everflame), the soot-stained alleys of the mercenary camps (where Black Banner captains drink with Vigil informers), and the basilicas where the Everflame's priests balance piety against profit (and sometimes sell indulgences for the sin of being born poor). The Grand Magistrate presides over a skeleton empire, but the real power moves in coin-houses, grain rolls, and the whispered judgments of the Censor's Hall --- and in the dreams of patricians who no longer remember the scent of rain.

The Vigil does not patrol the streets. It permeates them. Every tavern has an informer; every bathhouse has a spy; every marriage is witnessed by someone who reports to a faction of the Vigil. The Censer-Knights hunt unlicensed magic; the Unburnt hunt sedition; the Red Vigil hunts what was forgotten. They are not allies. They are not enemies. They are three creditors fighting over the same soul --- and the soul is the city's.

The patricians --- the Old Blood --- no longer govern. They perform governance. A session of the Parlement might see two senators debate the price of grain while their slaves hold silver trays of honeyed figs. A decree might be passed that doubles the salt tax, then rescinded an hour later because a duchess sneezed. They are not cruel. They are distracted. Livaea has taught them to crave the next pleasure before they have finished the current one. Maelstraeus has taught them that every transaction is a game, and every game is winnable if you have enough coin --- but they have forgotten that the house always wins. The house is the Hollow.

The Everflame still burns in the basilicas, but its light has grown dim. The High Priest speaks of unity, but his sermons are interrupted by coughing from the pews --- not from illness, but from the ash that falls like snow. The common folk whisper that the flame no longer judges truth; it only records it, filing confessions in a vault that no one ever opens. The Vigil has a key. The patricians have a copy. The poor have nothing but their names, which the Censor's Hall writes in ink that fades when you look away.

*{The Everflame --- Faith of Ash and Incense The state religion of Ecktoria is a syncretic faith that venerates the Eternal Flame as the divine principle, but its power flows through three great Orders, each devoted to a different Patron. The Basilica of the Unquenched Flame is the heart of the city: a cavernous space of gold mosaics, weeping saints, and the perpetual smell of beeswax and myrrh. Mass is held at dawn and dusk, the congregation coughing through the ash that falls from the censers. The priests sell indulgences in the side chapels --- a silver coin for the remission of a small sin, a gold coin for a large one, a horse for a murder. The choir sings in Utaran, a language most parishioners no longer understand, but the melody is beautiful enough to make them weep --- and to make the statues turn their heads.

itemize Order of the Oath of Flame & Light (Orthodox) --- The public face of the church. They lead processions, bless marriages, and anoint the dying. Their rites are those of vows, radiance, and protection. They are the ones who light the great candles at dawn. Many are sincere; most are tired. The tired ones are the ones the Vigil watches. Order of the Inquisitor Prime (Prosecutorial) --- The Censer-Knights. They hunt untutored magic, root out demonic cults, and enforce the purity of the flame. Their rites are those of judgment, purification, and burning censure. They carry brass censers that smoke with bitter herbs, and bells that ring once for a warning, thrice for a raid. They are feared, but also respected --- for they are the only ones who still take the flame seriously. And the flame takes them seriously in return. Order of the Oath of Mercy and Grace (Hospitalier) --- The healers and confessors. They run hospices, tend to the sick, and hear the confessions of the dying. Their rites are those of healing, forgiveness, and the spiral of care. They are often at odds with the Inquisitor Prime --- mercy can look like leniency. The common folk love them; the patricians ignore them. The Vigil watches both. itemize

The Everflame is both beautiful and ugly. Its gold leaf covers the same marble where petitioners once scratched their names. Its hymns are sung by choirboys whose families sold them for a loaf of bread. Its relics --- a nail from the True Cross, a tear of Saint Lucia --- draw pilgrims from across the empire, but the priests charge admission, and the poor sleep on the steps. The flame itself is a wonder: a pillar of blue-white fire that never gutters, tended by the High Priest alone. It is said to show the truth to any who stare into it long enough --- and to show the same truth to the Vigil, who watch from the shadows. The truth is always the same: you have forgotten something.

*{At a Glance itemize[leftmargin=*] Atmosphere: Marble grandeur, ash-tipped ambition, the slow rot of imperial forms, and the cloying sweetness of a city drowning in its own nostalgia. Key Conflict: Legitimacy versus wealth; old families, new mercenaries, and the Everflame's judgment --- but beneath it all, the battle between Maelstraeus (greed) and Livaea (desire) over the souls of the powerful. Notable Threats: Bread riots, arena spills, ducal conflicts, inquisitorial visits, the Red List --- and the Vigil's three factions, who may arrest you for unpaid weights, sedition, or simply being remembered. Common Rewards: Procession permits, coin-house credit, laurel deeds, a Golden Edict carved in precedent --- and, if you are brave, a secret from the Vigil's own files. The secret is always about a statue. itemize

*{Prominent NPCs of Ecktoria longtable{|p{0.2|p{0.25|p{0.3|p{0.15|

Name & Role/Title & Motivation & Rumored Quirk

Valerius & Grand Magistrate & Preserve the forms of law while the empire crumbles & His gavel is a sundial; it only strikes at noon. He has not struck it in a year.

Flavia & High Priest of the Everflame & Balance the factions and keep the flame lit & She has never raised her voice; the flame speaks for her. Lately, it speaks in the voices of the dead.

Cassia & Duchess-Regent of the Dolmis Gate & Protect her family's trading rights & She rules from behind a silk screen; a dagger-hole is cut at eye level. The screen was a gift from Livaea. The silk is warm.

Jackson & Coin-House Factor & Survive the next trust panic & He seals deals with three ribbons: white (ask), red (now), black (never). His black ribbon is fraying. He does not know who is fraying it.

Lucius & Lanista (arena master) & Own the next champion and the secrets of the hypogeum & His fighters' brands are his signature; he never repeats a mark. One brand is a spiral --- the mark of the Red Vigil. The fighter does not know.

The Red Clerk & Censor's Hall scribe & Control which names are remembered & His inkwell is a skull; the skull belonged to a name he erased. He has forgotten his own name. The skull remembers.

Censer-Knight Commander Vorenus & Prefect of the Order of the Inquisitor Prime & Hunt untutored magic before it wakes the Ninth & His lantern burns green. He says it shows the truth. His shadow has two heads --- one for each faction he has betrayed. He does not notice the third.

Lady Aelia & Patrician heiress, Livaea's favorite & Outshine every rival before the next masque & She has not left the Amber Palace in seven years. She does not know the price of bread. She dreams of bread.

longtable

*{Names of Ecktoria Inspired by late Latin and early Romance: strong vowels (ae, i, o), soft endings, and the weight of imperial bureaucracy. Surnames often denote a place of origin, a trade, or a notable ancestor.

longtable{|p{0.25|p{0.25|p{0.2|p{0.2|

First Names (Male) & First Names (Female) & Surnames & Epithets

Valerius & Flavia & de Urbe & the Iron

Jackson & Cassia & Aquilinus & the Coin-Wise

Lucius & Aelia & Honoratus & Flame-Touched

Tiberius & Cornelia & Lateranus & the Unburned

Decimus & Valeria & Portinari & Ash-Finger

longtable

*{The Three Ecktorias: Faction Spectrum Ecktoria is not a single polity but a battlefield of three competing visions. Most citizens and factions fall somewhere between these poles, shifting allegiance as the grain price changes. Superimposed on them are the whispers of Maelstraeus and Livaea, who have no faction --- only those who owe.

itemize[leftmargin=*] Old Blood (Patrician Traditionalists) -- The ancient senatorial families who remember when Ecktoria ruled the world. They worship precedent, hoard marble, and see mercenaries as necessary filth. Their power is in law, lineage, and the silent networks of the Censor's Hall. Timers: Patrician Patience [4], Blood Price [6]. Patron whispers: Livaea --- they marry for influence, betray for pleasure, and mistake cruelty for wit. They mistake silence for consent.

Black Purse (Mercenary & Trade Leagues) -- The new power: condotta brokers, coin-house factors, and mercenary captains who have bought what birth could not give. They care about supply lines, not bloodlines. Their loyalty is to the next contract, and their god is the tally --- but the tally has no god. Timers: Contract Spiral [4], Payday Fever [6]. Patron whispers: Maelstraeus --- they measure profit in margins, margins in suffering, and suffering in silver. They do not measure the weight of marble.

The Everflame (Church & Inquisition) -- The priests, inquisitors, and relic-keepers who claim that the Eternal Flame is the only true sovereign. They offer legitimacy to whoever pays the tithe --- and withdraw it from whoever forgets. Their power is in pulpits, processions, and the fear of excommunication. Timers: Heresy Index [4], Sanctuary Violation [6]. Patron whispers: Neither --- the Everflame remembers, but the Vigil has learned to filter its truths. The flame's truth is ash; the Vigil's truth is silence. itemize

Superimposed on these are the Ykrul creditors (who hold Ecktoria's weight like a leash), the Black Banners (who hold its streets like a lease), and the Vigil (who hold its secrets like a knife --- and sometimes use it). A wise politician learns to balance all three --- and keeps a boat ready on the Dolmis.

*{The Vigil --- Three Faces of the Secret Police The Vigil is not one order but three, each claiming the mantle of the old imperial secret police. They do not cooperate. They do not war. They audit each other. They also audit the dead.

itemize[leftmargin=*] The Censer-Knights (Orthodox, Everflame) --- Hunters of untutored magic. They carry brass censers that smoke with bitter herbs and bells that ring once for a warning, thrice for a raid. They answer to the Synod and the High Priest, but their true loyalty is to the precedent --- the law that magic must be licensed, indexed, and taxed. A Censer-Knight raid is heralded by a single, low bell. They are the most feared and the most public. They are also the most forgetful. Timers: Candle-Smoke Sweep [4], Heretic Index [6].

The Unburnt (Patrician) --- Bodyguards and spies of the Amber Palace. They wear red sashes and carry no visible weapons. Their job is to ensure that no whisper of rebellion, no bread riot, no unpaid toll ever reaches a patrician's ear. They are experts at redaction --- not killing witnesses, but making them forget. They have forgotten their own names. Timers: Palace Secrets [4], Redacted Memory [6].

The Red Vigil (Maelstraeus's Own) --- Collectors and enforcers. They serve no noble, no church, no magistrate --- only the record. They audit the wealthy for the wealthy, finding hidden obligations, unpaid tithes, and compound tolls that have grown teeth. A Red Vigil agent never demands coin. They demand names. They also demand silence --- the silence of the forgotten. Timers: Toll Due [4], Roll of Names [6]. itemize

The Vigil's three commanders meet once a month in the hypogeum of the Arena, under the gaze of statues that weep marble tears. No record is kept of these meetings. No one admits they happen. The common folk say that if you see a Vigil agent, it is already too late --- but they cannot agree on which Vigil. The statues know. The statues do not tell.

*{Spades --- Places (revised tone) enumerate

Petition Steps --- Milk-stone worn smooth by a thousand hopeful knees. The petitions are written in ink and wax. The steps answer in silence, and the silence is a verdict: "Your claim is under review." A clerk sweeps the steps at dawn. He never reads the petitions. He weighs them. The heaviest goes to the bottom of the pile. The pile is the city's memory. Tally-Ford Ferryhouse --- Rope-drums thrum all day; the ferrymaster watches coin and banners. The rope-drums have a pattern. It spells the names of those who paid late --- and the names of those who forgot they owed. The ferrymaster has a brother in the Red Vigil. They do not speak. Everflame Cloister --- Candle markets and side-chapels. The flame has a voice. It is the voice of the last liar who tested it. Lately, it speaks in a whisper. The whisper is always the same: "I remember. I remember. I remember." Those who listen too long forget their own names and start reciting the names of the city's forgotten dead. Triumph Stairs --- Marble worn smooth by ambition. The stairs have a name: "The Rise of the Forgotten." No one remembers why. The patricians still climb them at noon, their retainers counting the steps aloud. They never count to nine. The ninth step is a gap. The gap is where a senator fell --- or was pushed --- or simply forgot to step. His ghost is still climbing. He has been climbing for a century. Censor's Hall --- Red-lacquered tablets record every name, every shame. The tablets are redacted in invisible ink. The truth is underneath. The Vigil has the reagent. The reagent is made from the tears of the last censor who told the truth. He is still crying. enumerate

*{Heads --- People & Factions (revised tone) enumerate

Torchbearer Child --- Ash-smudged hands, knowing every shortcut through the ruins. The ruins are not old. They are last year's fashionable quarter, abandoned when the mortgage was "reclassified." She has never been lost. The ruins speak to her in the language of eviction notices. She reads them aloud to the rats. The rats pay her in stolen bread. Decumanus-Master --- Surveyor who "moves" stones. His rod is a finger bone. The bone belonged to the last surveyor who argued. He does not rewrite the map. He rewrites the memory of the map. "This street was always a cul-de-sac. You simply forgot." The Vigil pays him in forgotten names. Censer-Knight Commander Vorenus --- His lantern burns green. He says it shows the truth. His shadow has two heads --- one for each faction he has betrayed. He does not see the third head. The third head is his own, from a timeline where he kept his oath. It is weeping. enumerate

*{Clubs --- Complications/Threats (revised tone) enumerate

Blocked Bridge --- A funeral procession for a pauper who died in debt. The law says the corpse cannot cross until the toll is paid. The family has no coin. The law does not accept stories. The corpse is beginning to smell, and the Hollow is beginning to notice a weight that cannot be buried. The Vigil is taking notes on who stops to help. Border Dispute --- A surveyor has moved a boundary stone three feet east. The new map shows your warehouse now stands on a public thoroughfare. The old map is in the archive. The archive wing collapsed last spring. The memory of the boundary now exists only in the cracked floor of the basilica, and the basilica is accepting sealed bids for "reparative memory." The Vigil has already bid. enumerate

*{Diamonds --- Rewards/Leverage (revised tone) enumerate

Procession Permit --- Move sacred flame through a crowded quarter; law walks with you. The permit is a torch. Carry it, and the city will part --- or burn. The Vigil will follow. The flame will flicker when you lie. The permit is signed by a clerk who died three years ago. His ghost still signs. Golden Edict --- An exception carved in marble; precedent travels like law. The edict is a crack. The crack in the law is now an opening. The Vigil is already inside. They will leave a note. The note will say: "We remember. Do you?" enumerate

*{Quick use notes itemize Draw until you have all four suits: Spade = place, Heart = actor, Club = pressure, Diamond = leverage. Highest rank sets the main Timer (2--5 4, 6--10 6, J/Q/K 8, A 10). Diamonds are codified outcomes (papers/favor/precedence) that change position rather than call for a roll. If any A appears, echo marble and fire motifs --- statues that turn their heads, flames that burn blue, law that remembers every name --- and also echo the Vigil's presence: a cough in the crowd, a seal on a document you did not stamp, a mirror that shows a different face. The different face is always older. It is always watching. itemize

*{Additional Features (Cultural Mechanics)

itemize Imperial Ruins: Every district bears half-collapsed arcades, serving as markets, courts, or shelters for mercenary companies. The Vigil knows every hidden passage. Competing Factions: Dukes, guilds, and priests stage processions that collide at bridges and squares, each claiming legitimacy. The Vigil collects bets on the outcome. Monuments as Weapons: Statues, obelisks, and colonnades are not only symbols --- they are barricades, rallying points, and high ground when the city riots. The Vigil has a map of which statues can be toppled. Everflame Orthodoxy: The basilicas preach unity, but each sermon is a coded signal for a political camp. The High Priest plays balance while cultivating his own following. The Vigil decodes every sermon. Black Banner Contracts: Foreign mercenaries fill the gaps left by a shrinking citizen levy, their loyalty secured only by steady coin. The Vigil audits their pay. The Amber Palace Quarantine: The patricians have not left the palace in a decade. No one is certain if they are prisoners or patrons. The Vigil maintains the perimeter --- and delivers the wine. Local Patrons (Syncretic Saints & Cults): itemize St. Marcellus of the Gilded Scale (Mykkiel) --- The Justiciar. His order enforces unwritten laws; reading a sealed precedent binds the reader to enforce it. His shrine is the Censor's Hall. The Vigil keeps a copy of every precedent. St. Valeria of the Serpent (Isoka) --- The Shedding Saint. Pilgrims who confess at her chapel forget one sin --- and one kindness. Her mosaic shows a snake eating its tail. The Vigil uses her chapel to erase memories. St. Lucia of the Undying Flame (Oath of Flame & Light) --- The Witness of Embers. Any oath sworn over her shrine's flame cannot be broken; the breaker sees their own face in every wave until they drown. The Vigil swears oaths there --- and never breaks them. The Reckoner (Maelstraeus) --- The Tally Keeper. A mystery cult of coin-house factors. They offer credit at the cost of a hidden toll that compounds in blood. The Red Vigil are their collectors. The Ember Prefect (Heretic Cult) --- A schism that believes the flame should not be contained. They meet in the arena hypogeum, chanting the Unspoken Ninth Citation. The Grand Magistrate has outlawed them three times. The Vigil has infiltrated them twice. The Velvet Mask (Livaea) --- A secret society of patricians who worship pleasure as the only truth. Their rites are said to last nine days. No one has ever left a rite unchanged. The Vigil watches their parties --- and reports to the Unburnt. itemize itemize

*{Lore Echoes (Cross-Expansion Hooks) itemize The Bitter Root: Daughters of the Cord run a hospice in the Shatterline Bazaar. They ask no payment --- but they cut threads. A renegade Sister hides among the arena slaves, trying to untie her cord from a coin-house weight. The Vigil has her file. Malachai, the Chained Angel: A silver coin that never spends circulates among the grain-prefects. Take it, and your store never runs dry --- but the coin will reappear in the mouth of a starving child. The Red Vigil uses it to mark those who owe. The Gray Wanderer's Grimoire: Ecktorian psions are called "Flame-Readers." They see the future in the Everflame's flicker. The High Priest employs three --- and has never trusted a forecast that didn't smell of smoke. The Vigil employs their apprentices. The Threshold Compendium: The Imperial Arena is a Class IV Poltergeist site --- the collective rage of every gladiator who died for the crowd. Saikou Ira would ring a bell eight times and leave a wreath of laurel. The Vigil rings the bell nine times. The Arena answers. The Ninth: In any Ecktorian court, the ninth citation of precedent voids all previous. The wise stop at eight and settle. Those who cite the ninth hear a faint laughter from the marble walls --- and their cases are never heard again. The Vigil cites the ninth often. The laughter does not bother them. itemize

*{Ecktoria Superstitions (burn for +1 die once) itemize Bread to the Flame: Crumble a crust into the Everflame. Ignore the first Bread Riots penalty. Knot the Ribbon: Tie a single knot in a coin-house credit ribbon. Cancel Frozen Purse or take --1 die on your next trust roll (your choice). Name to the Marble: Whisper a dead debtor's name into a statue's ear. Gain +1 Effect when collecting from their heirs this scene, but mark Obligation to the Grand Magistrate. Spit on the Seal: Before signing any contract, spit on the wax seal. If it sizzles, the Vigil has a copy. itemize

*{Thematic SB Spend Table

Minor Complications (1 SB) itemize Exposure: Your actions draw unwanted attention from city guards or rival factions. Noise: Sounds of your actions alert nearby crowds or ceremonial processions. Trace: Evidence of your passage marks your route for patrols or inquisitors. Delay: A brief but meaningful setback costs you time or ceremonial opportunity. Supply Strain: Mark +1 segment on a relevant resource timer. itemize

Moderate Setbacks (2 SB) itemize Alarm Raised: City authorities or noble house becomes aware and begins responding. Position Lost: You lose advantageous ground/cover/stealth due to crowd movement or procession. Foe Appears: A rival faction member or city official arrives on scene. Gear Trouble: A piece of equipment becomes Compromised/Neglected. Lock/Barrier: A simple obstacle now requires a test to overcome. itemize

Serious Trouble (3 SB) itemize Reinforcements: Additional guards, mercenaries, or zealots arrive. Key Gear Breaks: A crucial tool/weapon becomes temporarily unusable. Major Twist: The situation fundamentally changes --- procession turns violent/boundary dispute erupts/patron turns. Rail Tick: Advance a relevant campaign/front timer by 1 segment. Condition Applied: Mark Fatigue 1/Harm 1/Condition appropriate to fiction. itemize

Major Turns (4+ SB) itemize Trap Springs: A prepared danger activates with full effect. Authority Arrival: High Priest, Grand Magistrate, or Duchess-Regent intervenes. Scene Shift: The environment changes dramatically --- procession erupts/arena gate opens/monument collapses. Patron Omen: Divine/arcane forces take notice --- flame speaks/blessing lost/curse manifests. Narrative Pivot: The story takes an unexpected turn that reframes objectives. itemize

Region-Specific SB Options itemize Ecktoria (Marble & Fire): Statues turn their heads, flames burn blue, ceremonial masks appear in windows. Ecktoria (Procession): Crowds chant your name, banners block your path, trumpets announce your arrival. Ecktoria (Patronage): Seals break in your hand, favors are revoked, noble houses compete for your allegiance. Ecktoria (The Vigil): A seal appears on your documents that you did not stamp. A cough from the crowd sounds like your name. Your reflection in a polished shield has a red sash. itemize

*{Extensions (Plug-in)

itemize Cross-Region Hooks (optional draws): itemize Aeler True Masons bid to shore a failing aqueduct; accepting stabilizes Aftershock but grants them soft leverage in the ward. The Vigil audits the contract. Aelinnel Green-Market broker offers "truth-credit" on court gossip; pay later in favors or names. The Vigil pays in silence. Black Banner retainer parade demands back pay on the Triumph Stairs; crowd sympathy tilts your next parley. The Vigil arrests the ringleader. itemize

Faction Entanglements During Travel (use en route or on city entry): itemize If the party holds any Condotta Diamonds, begin with Bread Riots at +1 segment (mercenary grain trains re-routed). Entering with an Underway Pass (Aeler) grants +1 Position on Aqueduct Walk scenes but ticks Ducal Conflict once (guild jealousy). The Vigil notes your pass. Carrying Green Market tags downgrades one Censor's Hall action but flags Inquisitorial Visit at the next temple. The Vigil compares tags. itemize

Trade Good Consequences (pick 1 when a haul sells): itemize Arena Steel street duels spike start Arena Spill at 1. The Vigil collects the weapons. Relic Fragments basilica prestige swings tick Church Purge or grant Basilica Audience. The Vigil inspects the relic. Survey Rights boundary re-plat downgrade one Border Dispute or upgrade a rival's. The Vigil updates the map. itemize

Diplomatic Favor (minor currency): itemize Earn by resolving a public scene without bloodshed (Forum, Procession, Coin-House). Spend 1 to reduce DV of a permit/audience by 1, or to ignore Blocked Bridge for one crossing. Holding 2+ makes you "interesting": start a Red List [4] that advances on snubbed patrons. The Vigil adds your name. itemize

Reputation Echoes (soft power): itemize Crowd-Loved (Laurel-Deed): first time per session you face a mob, set Position one step safer. The Vigil watches how the mob loves you. Coin-House Trusted (Credit): ignore the first Frozen Purse; next time, it returns at +1 severity. The Vigil audits your credit. Clerically Vouched (Everflame): downgrade one Inquisitorial Visit; if abused, promote Church Purge. The Vigil has a copy of your voucher. itemize itemize

*{Monuments as Mechanics (opulence with teeth) itemize Processional Geometry: When acting on the Triumph Stairs, choose: +1 Effect or --1 Heat; on a miss, Rerouted Triumph advances. The Vigil stands at the top of the stairs, watching. Aqueduct Leverage: Securing an aqueduct span grants a one-scene high ground tag in any linked ward; if collateral hits, tick Aftershock. The Vigil knows where the aqueduct leaks. Forum Acoustics: Rally speeches at the Grand Forum grant an extra assist die if at least 20 witnesses; failure spawns Bread Riots at +1. The Vigil counts the witnesses. itemize

*{Plug-in SB Conversions (Ecktoria flavor) itemize Any 1 SB may become Crowd Drift: shift the scene's terrain in favor of or against you (bridges clog, stairs clear). Any 2 SB may become Seal Revoked: void one paper (Permit/Chit/Indulgence); to avert, sacrifice Diplomatic Favor. On 3+ SB offer Patron Bargain: accept a visible mark (ribbon, laurel brand) to halve consequences; start a Patron Weight [6]. itemize

*{Hidden Orders & Ancient Conspiracies (d12 hooks)

enumerate The Ash Senate -- a roll of "extinguished" patrician lines still voting by proxy through funerary guilds. The Vigil has the key. Surveyors of the Ninth Mile -- decumanus-masters who can move boundaries with ritual math; a lost rod sets precedent across the city. The Vigil owns the rod. Lantern Tetrarchy -- four basilica beadles who decide which miracles are counted; the fifth lamp was broken in a purge. The Vigil lit the purge. The Red Marble Compact -- coin-house founders who tithe 1 in 100 coins to a secret indemnity fund used to flip coups. The Red Vigil administers the fund. Archivum Umbrae -- censor clerks maintaining a shadow index of names that must not stand trial (yet). The Vigil reads the index nightly. Peregrine Brotherhood -- ferrymasters trading in sealed confessions; blackmail that floats. The Vigil buys the confessions. The Laurel of Dust -- a gladiators' mutual that chooses which champions live long enough to hold office. The Vigil chooses the champions. Aqueduct Collegium Obscura -- engineers who keep a second set of sluice keys; droughts become elections. The Vigil has the third set. Order of the Broken Obelisk -- survey-cult that believes Mile-Zero is wrong and can re-found the city with one stone. The Vigil has the stone. The Funeral Republic -- undertakers running a parallel courier state through catacombs and wakes. The Vigil uses their couriers. The Ember Vault -- a sealed reliquary under the Arena; victors' sweat was bottled for augury. The Vigil broke the seal. Triumphal Maskers -- procession costumers who encode heretical theses into robe pleats and torch patterns. The Vigil decodes the theses --- and sometimes agrees. enumerate

*{Cults & Mystery Rites (d10)

enumerate Children of the Phoenix (rebirth by fire): ash-marks grant +1 die vs. fear in crowds; mark Fatigue if you hesitate near open flame. Verdant Laurel (blood to marble, sap to coin): once/session turn 1 Harm into 1 Coin-House Credit; start a Green Weight [4]. Mask of the Thirteenth (forbidden procession step): walk through a riot unmolested; next parley with clergy begins one step worse. Brotherhood of the River Tongue (truth ferried at night): ask the Dolmis one yes/no; a ferryman shows up later to collect a name. Candlemakers of Ever (flame remembers lies): blue flame points to falsehood; the liar dreams of you for three nights (Exposure +1). Sable Standard (war saint cult): your banner ignores morale checks once; start a Blood-Tithe [4] owed to the saint's chapel. The Ninth Bell (hour that is not kept): skip a queue or sentence; next edict you need arrives late (Delay SB free once). Guild of Red Accountants (sin as tally): convert 2 SB into a Magistrate's Postponement; add Karmic Weight [6]. The Marble Eaters (mortification sect): bite stone, gain +1 Effect resisting crowd crush; take Harm 1 (Stress) if you boast. Torch of Saint Vessa (saint of necessary arson): sanctify a fire to purge a tag (Plague/Corruption); City Heat +2. enumerate

*{Religious Schisms & Trials

The Everflame Divide (choose a live schism) enumerate Orthodox Basilica vs. Ember Reformers: Is miracle counted by law or kindled by need? Laurel Theologians vs. Street Saints: Does glory flow down from triumphs or up from bread-lines? Iconoclast Ward vs. Reliquary Keepers: Is the flame purer without images? enumerate

Schism Mechanics (table-facing) itemize Heresy Index [0--6]: ticks when players publicize a stance, defy clergy, or wield cult rites in view. At 3: inquisitorial audits become common; at 6: Church Purge (Q) auto-triggers once/session until lowered. Sanctuary / Anathema Tags: locations acquire tags during arc scenes. Sanctuary: first harm in scene becomes Fatigue; Anathema: first social miss spawns "Proscribed Sigil." Trial by Fire (mini-procedure, 3 beats): enumerate Assembly: set the forum/stair as arena; state thesis in one sentence (players or NPCs). Witness: three proofs (relic, miracle, precedent). Each proof: test Social/Arcana/Resolve vs. crowd DV; on success, reduce Index by 1 or gain Basilica Audience. Judgment: roll Crowd Mood (1d6): 1--2 mobs, 3--4 silent acceptance, 5--6 laurel toss (gain Laurel-Deed). If Index 4, --1 to the roll. enumerate itemize

*{Ecktoria -- Marble and Fire Starting Location: The Coin-House floor, marble cracked with soot lines, where votive candles burn beside melted icons. quote "In Ecktoria, even faith must balance its ledgers, and the gods audit in ash." quote In the Coin-House floor, a factor with smoke-singed rolls offers a purse. "The Lame King moves," she says. "And the Basilica burns with him. The Amber Palace has not answered a summons in a month. The Vigil is asking questions. I suggest you find the answers before they find you."

Regional Motifs Coin-oaths, basilica relics, marble acoustics, augury smoke, Vigil seals. Whispers to Offer "Bid against the city," "Pocket the relic shard," "Name an innocent debtor," "The Vigil's informer sits in the corner." External Timers Threat: Procession of Ash [6]; Community: Credit Panic [4]; Anchor: Basilica Choir [4]; Vigil: Red List [6]. Complications Smoke speaks truths; coins stick to skin; a reliquary runs hot; a seal appears on your documents that you did not stamp. Anchors at Risk A choir novice; a patron account; a Vigil informer who owes you a favor. Escalation Beat The Basilica tolls a note only one PC hears. The Vigil hears it too. Relief Return the shard; pay a city-toll in blood or Boons; route the procession elsewhere; give the Vigil a name.

*{Boss Seeds (Cults & Conspiracies) itemize The Ember Prefect (schismatic inquisitor): Phase [8] Citation Censure Auto-da-fé Purge. Lair: Everflame Cloister. Counters: relic provenance, crowd-favor, rival sermon. Vigil twist: The Prefect was once a Vigil informer. The Vigil knows where he hides. The Surveyor-Primus (geomantic usurper): Phase [6] Re-plat Dispossess Erase. Lair: Mile-Zero. Counters: Aeler measure-rods, witness line, broken rod. Vigil twist: The Primus has a Red Vigil agent as a protégé. Phoenix of the Arena (miracle or hoax?): Phase [10] Hype Immolation "Rebirth" Riot. Lair: Imperial Arena. Counters: doused ash, Mask of the Thirteenth, Everflame test. Vigil twist: The Phoenix is a Vigil experiment. The Unburnt want to contain it. The Red Vigil wants to sell tickets. The Velvet Mask (Livaea's cult): Phase [8] Invitation Masque Price of Pleasure. Lair: The Amber Palace. Counters: an unbroken oath, a mirror with no reflection, a name spoken without desire. Vigil twist: The Vigil's Unburnt faction attends every masque. They are not there to stop it. itemize

*{Diamonds: Forbidden Leverage (add to :ecktoria-rewards) enumerate

Shadow Index Leaf -- remove one name from the Red List for a week; add Countercoup +1. Ember Writ -- sanction one cult rite as "orthodox" for a scene. Surveyor's Ninth Rod -- redraw one ward line unnoticed (until dawn). Vigil Seal (Red) -- For one scene, the Red Vigil treats you as a contractor. The price will be collected later. enumerate

*{Cross-Contamination (fast ties) itemize Aeler True Masons: backing an iconoclast audit grants shoring crews; bell-codes now gate temple entries. The Vigil has the bell-codes. Aelinnel Thorn Courts: hawthorn chaplains host "mirror sermons"; a basilica gains Sanctuary while a rival becomes Anathema. The Vigil watches both. Black Banners: war-chaplains monetize indulgences; clearing Payday risks +1 Heresy Index. The Vigil takes a cut. itemize

[colback=black!3,colframe=black!40!white,title={Patronage & Power,breakable] Ecktoria's decline is not collapse, but transformation. Wealth bleeds into patronage webs: a senator's indulgence, a censor's indulgence, a noble's purse. The city's great houses keep mercenary captains on retainer, while priests sell ritual processions as public theater and private favor. And everywhere, the Vigil watches --- not to stop the rot, but to take notes.

Yet beneath the decay, two demons whisper. Maelstraeus teaches that every contract is a weapon, every obligation a leash. Livaea teaches that every desire is a chain, every pleasure a payment. The patricians who once ruled the world now rule only the Amber Palace, and the Amber Palace is a gilded cage. They do not see the bread riots. They do not hear the Vigil's footsteps. They dance. They drink. They forget that the flame remembers everything.

For the GM: Patronage should function as both opportunity and trap. Any gift (a purse, permit, or charter) binds the receiver to service, but patrons can die, fall from favor, or suddenly proscribe what they once allowed. The Vigil offers a different kind of patronage: protection in exchange for information. A party that pays the Vigil in secrets may find doors opening --- and other doors closing behind them. To emphasize the decadence and dread: itemize Tie rewards to visible symbols (seals, ribbons, laurel crowns, Vigil marks) that can be stolen, forged, or voided. Let rival patrons issue conflicting "safe-conducts," forcing players to choose whose favor matters more. Use processions, funerals, or trials as arenas for social contests, where patronage weights surface and shift allegiances. Introduce the Vigil's Faction Timer [6] to track which Vigil faction (Censer-Knights, Unburnt, Red Vigil) has the most leverage over the party. When it fills, that faction makes a demand. itemize Patronage in Ecktoria is not mere background --- it is the engine of politics, coin, and survival. And beneath it all, Maelstraeus and Livaea are writing a contract. The city is the collateral. The poor are the interest. The patricians are the signature --- and they signed in blood long ago.
